questions.q: Confirmation Rate. Signups(user_id PK, time_stamp), Confirmations(user_id, time_stamp, action ENUM('confirmed','timeout')). The confirmation rate of a user is the number of 'confirmed' messages divided by total requests. Report user_id and confirmation_rate rounded to 2 decimals (0 if no requests).

answer.oneLiner: LEFT JOIN signups to confirmations; AVG of (action='confirmed') gives the rate, wrapped in IFNULL/ROUND for users with no requests.

code.snippet:
SELECT s.user_id,
       ROUND(IFNULL(AVG(c.action = 'confirmed'), 0), 2) AS confirmation_rate
FROM Signups s
LEFT JOIN Confirmations c ON s.user_id = c.user_id
GROUP BY s.user_id;
code.note: AVG of a boolean = fraction true; IFNULL handles no-request users

keyPoints: (c.action = 'confirmed') yields 1/0; AVG = confirmation fraction.
IFNULL(..., 0) covers users with no confirmations.

dialogue.text: Compute each user's confirmation rate = confirmed / total requests, 0 if none.
I'd LEFT JOIN Signups to Confirmations and take AVG(c.action = 'confirmed'), which averages 1s and 0s into the fraction.
What about users with no requests at all?
Their AVG is NULL, so I wrap it in IFNULL(..., 0) and ROUND to two decimals.
Why does AVG of a boolean give the rate?
In MySQL a boolean evaluates to 1 or 0, so its average is exactly the proportion that are true.

followUpsQA.q: Why does AVG of a boolean give the rate?
followUpsQA.a: True/false become 1/0, so the average is the fraction true.
Users with no requests have NULL average; default to 0.
To keep users who never had a confirmation row.

questions.q: Not Boring Movies. Cinema(id PK, movie, description, rating). Report movies with an odd id and a description that is not 'boring', ordered by rating descending.

answer.oneLiner: Filter id % 2 = 1 and description <> 'boring', then ORDER BY rating DESC.

code.snippet:
SELECT *
FROM Cinema
WHERE id % 2 = 1 AND description <> 'boring'
ORDER BY rating DESC;
code.note: Modulo for odd ids; ORDER BY DESC

keyPoints: id % 2 = 1 selects odd ids.

dialogue.text: Report movies with an odd id and description not 'boring', ordered by rating descending.
I'd filter WHERE id % 2 = 1 AND description <> 'boring', then ORDER BY rating DESC.
How are you testing for an odd id?
id % 2 = 1 picks odd ids; MOD(id, 2) = 1 is the portable equivalent.
Does <> 'boring' exclude any rows it shouldn't?
Only exact 'boring' descriptions are excluded; here description is non-NULL so the comparison is straightforward.

followUpsQA.q: How do you select odd ids?
followUpsQA.a: id % 2 = 1, or MOD(id, 2) = 1.

followUpsQA.q: What if description could be NULL?
followUpsQA.a: Add OR description IS NULL since NULL <> 'boring' is UNKNOWN.
After the WHERE clause, sorting the filtered rows.

questions.q: Average Selling Price. Prices(product_id, start_date, end_date, price), UnitsSold(product_id, purchase_date, units). For each product compute the average selling price = SUM(price*units)/SUM(units), rounded to 2 decimals; products with no sales get 0.

answer.oneLiner: LEFT JOIN prices to units sold where the purchase date falls inside the price window, then take the weighted average, defaulting to 0.

code.snippet:
SELECT p.product_id,
       IFNULL(ROUND(SUM(p.price * u.units) / SUM(u.units), 2), 0) AS average_price
FROM Prices p
LEFT JOIN UnitsSold u
  ON p.product_id = u.product_id
 AND u.purchase_date BETWEEN p.start_date AND p.end_date
GROUP BY p.product_id;
code.note: Date-range join + weighted average; IFNULL for no-sales (division by NULL)

keyPoints: Join on purchase_date BETWEEN start and end.
Weighted avg = SUM(price*units)/SUM(units); IFNULL gives 0.

dialogue.text: Compute each product's average selling price = SUM(price*units)/SUM(units), 0 if no sales.
I'd LEFT JOIN Prices to UnitsSold where purchase_date falls BETWEEN start_date and end_date, then take the weighted average.
Why a date-range join condition?
Each price is valid for a window, so a unit's price depends on which window its purchase_date falls into.
What about products with no sales?
SUM(units) is NULL there, so the division is NULL; I wrap it in IFNULL(..., 0).

followUpsQA.q: Why join on a date range?
followUpsQA.a: Price is only valid within its start/end window.
No-sales products divide by NULL, defaulting to 0.

followUpsQA.q: Why weighted average not plain AVG?
followUpsQA.a: Different quantities sold must weight each price.

questions.q: Project Employees I. Project(project_id, employee_id), Employee(employee_id PK, name, experience_years). For each project report the average experience years of its employees, rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: Join Project to Employee on employee_id and AVG(experience_years) per project_id.

code.snippet:
SELECT p.project_id,
       ROUND(AVG(e.experience_years), 2) AS average_years
FROM Project p
JOIN Employee e ON p.employee_id = e.employee_id
GROUP BY p.project_id;
code.note: JOIN + AVG + ROUND grouped by project

keyPoints: Group by project_id, average the joined experience values.

dialogue.text: For each project, report the average experience years of its employees.
I'd JOIN Project to Employee on employee_id, GROUP BY project_id, and AVG(experience_years).
Why an inner join is fine here?
Every project row references a real employee, so there's nothing to preserve with a LEFT JOIN.
How do you get two decimals?
Wrap the AVG in ROUND(..., 2).

followUpsQA.a: To average experience within each project.
The problem requires two decimal places.
If a project could list a non-existent employee.

questions.q: Percentage of Users Attended a Contest. Users(user_id PK, user_name), Register(contest_id, user_id). For each contest, report the percentage of all users who registered, rounded to 2 decimals. Order by percentage descending, then contest_id ascending.

answer.oneLiner: Count distinct registrants per contest, divide by the total user count (a scalar subquery), and order as required.

code.snippet:
SELECT contest_id,
       ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) * 100.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Users), 2) AS percentage
FROM Register
GROUP BY contest_id
ORDER BY percentage DESC, contest_id ASC;
code.note: Scalar subquery for the denominator (total users)

keyPoints: Denominator is the full user count via a scalar subquery.
Multiply before dividing to keep precision.

dialogue.text: For each contest, report the percentage of all users who registered.
I'd GROUP BY contest_id and compute COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) * 100.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Users).
Why a scalar subquery for the denominator?
The denominator is the total user count, independent of the contest grouping, so a one-time scalar subquery is the cleanest source.
Any precision pitfall?
I multiply by 100.0 before dividing so it stays floating point, then ROUND to two decimals and order by percentage desc, contest_id asc.

followUpsQA.a: Total users is constant across contests, so compute it once.
To force float division and preserve decimals.
A user could register multiple rows; count them once per contest.

questions.q: Queries Quality and Percentage. Queries(query_name, result, position, rating). For each query_name compute quality = AVG(rating/position) and poor_query_percentage = percentage of queries with rating < 3, both rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: Group by query_name; quality is AVG(rating/position) and poor percentage is AVG(rating < 3) * 100.

code.snippet:
SELECT query_name,
       ROUND(AVG(rating / position), 2) AS quality,
       ROUND(AVG(rating < 3) * 100, 2) AS poor_query_percentage
FROM Queries
WHERE query_name IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY query_name;
code.note: AVG(rating < 3) averages a boolean to get the fraction

keyPoints: AVG(rating/position) is the average per-row ratio.
AVG(boolean)*100 gives the poor-query percentage.

dialogue.text: Per query_name, compute quality = AVG(rating/position) and the percent of rows with rating < 3.
I'd GROUP BY query_name, take AVG(rating / position) for quality, and AVG(rating < 3) * 100 for the poor percentage.
How does AVG(rating < 3) become a percentage?
rating < 3 evaluates to 1 or 0 per row, so its average is the fraction of poor queries; times 100 gives the percent.
Any rows to exclude?
I filter out NULL query_name, and round both metrics to two decimals.

followUpsQA.q: How does AVG(rating < 3) give a percentage?
followUpsQA.a: The boolean is 1/0, so the average times 100 is the percent.
NULL groups shouldn't appear in per-query metrics.
The average rating-to-position ratio per row.

questions.q: Nth Highest Salary. Employee(id PK, salary). Write a generic solution to return the Nth highest distinct salary (return NULL if it does not exist).

answer.oneLiner: Rank distinct salaries with DENSE_RANK() descending and pick rank N; equivalently use DISTINCT ... LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1.

code.note: DENSE_RANK dedupes ties; LIMIT/OFFSET works over a DISTINCT set

keyPoints: DENSE_RANK = N targets the Nth distinct salary.
Empty result naturally yields NULL.

dialogue.text: Write a generic solution for the N-th highest distinct salary, NULL if it doesn't exist.
I'd DENSE_RANK() salaries descending and pick rnk = N; DISTINCT salary in the outer select keeps ties collapsed.
What's the LIMIT/OFFSET alternative?
SELECT DISTINCT salary ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1; the DISTINCT is essential so duplicates don't shift the offset.
What if there are fewer than N distinct salaries?
The query returns no row, which surfaces as NULL, exactly what's required.

followUpsQA.a: DENSE_RANK collapses tied salaries; ROW_NUMBER would not.
So duplicate salaries don't push the offset onto the wrong value.

followUpsQA.q: What happens when N exceeds the distinct count?
followUpsQA.a: No row, returned as NULL.

questions.q: Monthly Transactions I. Transactions(id, country, state ENUM('approved','declined'), amount, trans_date). For each month and country report total transaction count and amount, plus approved count and approved amount.

answer.oneLiner: Group by month and country, then use conditional aggregation (SUM over a boolean) to fold the approved subset into the same scan.
keyPoints: Bucket the month with DATE_FORMAT(trans_date,'%Y-%m').
SUM(state='approved') counts approved rows; SUM(IF(state='approved',amount,0)) totals their amount.
One GROUP BY pass produces all four metrics.

code.snippet:
SELECT DATE_FORMAT(trans_date, '%Y-%m') AS month,
       country,
       COUNT(*)                                   AS trans_count,
       SUM(state = 'approved')                    AS approved_count,
       SUM(amount)                                AS trans_total_amount,
       SUM(IF(state = 'approved', amount, 0))     AS approved_total_amount
FROM Transactions
GROUP BY month, country;
code.note: Conditional aggregation avoids a second pass

dialogue.text: Per month and country, give total and approved transaction counts and amounts.
I group by the formatted month and country, then use conditional sums so approved metrics come from the same scan.
How do you count just the approved ones without a separate query?
SUM(state='approved') treats the boolean as 1/0, and SUM(IF(state='approved',amount,0)) totals only approved amounts.

followUpsQA.a: MONTH() drops the year, so different years would collide into the same bucket.
CASE WHEN state='approved' THEN amount ELSE 0 END works on any engine.

questions.q: Immediate Food Delivery II. Delivery(delivery_id PK, customer_id, order_date, customer_pref_delivery_date). An order is immediate when the preferred date equals the order date. Find the percentage of immediate orders among each customer's first order, rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: Restrict to each customer's earliest order, then average a boolean (immediate?) and scale to a percentage.
keyPoints: First order = MIN(order_date) per customer, matched with an IN tuple.
AVG(order_date = pref_date) is the immediate fraction.
Multiply by 100 and ROUND to 2 decimals.

code.snippet:
SELECT ROUND(AVG(order_date = customer_pref_delivery_date) * 100, 2) AS immediate_percentage
FROM Delivery
WHERE (customer_id, order_date) IN (
    SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date)
    FROM Delivery
    GROUP BY customer_id
);
code.note: Filter to first orders, then average the boolean

dialogue.text: What share of customers' first orders are immediate?
I filter to each customer's MIN(order_date), then AVG the boolean order_date = preferred_date and multiply by 100.
Why average a boolean instead of COUNT/COUNT?
AVG of a 1/0 boolean is exactly the fraction of true rows, so it collapses the count-over-count into one expression.

followUpsQA.q: Could a window function replace the subquery?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) and keep rn = 1.

followUpsQA.q: What if a customer has two orders on the same earliest date?
followUpsQA.a: The tuple-IN would keep both; the problem guarantees one first order, but ROW_NUMBER would force a single row.

questions.q: Game Play Analysis IV. Activity(player_id, device_id, event_date, games_played), PK (player_id, event_date). Report the fraction of players who logged in again the day immediately after their first login, rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: Find each player's first login, then count how many also have a row exactly one day later; divide by the total distinct players.
keyPoints: First login = MIN(event_date) per player.
Check existence of first_date + 1 day via a tuple IN / self-match.
Denominator is COUNT(DISTINCT player_id) over the whole table.

code.snippet:
SELECT ROUND(
         COUNT(DISTINCT a.player_id) /
         (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT player_id) FROM Activity),
       2) AS fraction
FROM Activity a
WHERE (a.player_id, a.event_date) IN (
    SELECT player_id, DATE_ADD(MIN(event_date), INTERVAL 1 DAY)
    FROM Activity
    GROUP BY player_id
);
code.note: Match consecutive-day logins against first login + 1

dialogue.text: What fraction of players come back the day after their first login?
I compute each player's first date, then keep activity rows that fall on first_date + 1 day, and divide that count by total players.
Why the tuple (player_id, event_date) in the IN?
It ties the +1-day check to the same player, so a return login by a different player can't be miscounted.

followUpsQA.q: How would you generalize to N-day retention?
followUpsQA.a: Replace INTERVAL 1 DAY with INTERVAL N DAY in the match.
LEAD(event_date) OVER (PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY event_date) and compare to first date + 1.

questions.q: Number of Unique Subjects Taught by Each Teacher. Teacher(teacher_id, subject_id, dept_id), PK (subject_id, dept_id). Count the distinct subjects each teacher teaches.

answer.oneLiner: Group by teacher and COUNT(DISTINCT subject_id) so the same subject in two departments isn't double-counted.
keyPoints: DISTINCT inside COUNT dedupes subject across departments.
Group by teacher_id.

code.note: COUNT(DISTINCT ...) is the whole trick

dialogue.text: How many unique subjects does each teacher teach?
Group by teacher_id and COUNT(DISTINCT subject_id), since one subject can appear under multiple departments.
What if you forgot DISTINCT?
You'd count subject/department rows, overstating teachers who teach the same subject in several departments.

followUpsQA.q: Why is plain COUNT(subject_id) wrong here?
followUpsQA.a: It counts rows, so a subject taught in two depts counts twice.
Yes — it's why duplicates by department exist, making DISTINCT necessary.

questions.q: User Activity for the Past 30 Days I. Activity(user_id, session_id, activity_date, activity_type). Find the daily active user count for the 30-day window ending 2019-07-27 inclusive (a user is active if they did anything that day).

answer.oneLiner: Filter to the half-open 30-day window, then group by date and COUNT(DISTINCT user_id).
keyPoints: Window: activity_date > '2019-06-27' AND <= '2019-07-27' (30 days inclusive of the end).
DISTINCT user_id so multiple sessions in a day count once.
Days with zero activity simply don't appear.

code.snippet:
SELECT activity_date AS day,
       COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS active_users
FROM Activity
WHERE activity_date > '2019-06-27'
  AND activity_date <= '2019-07-27'
GROUP BY activity_date;
code.note: Half-open range gives exactly 30 days

dialogue.text: Daily active users for the 30 days ending 2019-07-27.
I bound activity_date to that 30-day window and group by date with COUNT(DISTINCT user_id).
Why DISTINCT?
A user can open several sessions a day; without DISTINCT they'd be counted multiple times as 'active'.

followUpsQA.a: Both express the same 30-day window; the strict form mirrors 'end minus 29 days'.

followUpsQA.q: How to also show zero-activity days?
followUpsQA.a: Left join against a generated calendar/date table.

questions.q: Product Sales Analysis III. Sales(sale_id, product_id, year, quantity, price), Product(product_id PK, product_name). For each product report the first year it sold, plus that year's quantity and price.

answer.oneLiner: Keep the row whose (product_id, year) matches the product's MIN(year); a tuple-IN handles ties when a product first sold in one year across multiple rows.
keyPoints: First year = MIN(year) per product_id.
Match the full row with a (product_id, year) IN subquery.
Window RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY year) is an equivalent approach.

code.snippet:
SELECT s.product_id, s.year AS first_year, s.quantity, s.price
FROM Sales s
WHERE (s.product_id, s.year) IN (
    SELECT product_id, MIN(year)
    FROM Sales
    GROUP BY product_id
);
code.note: Tuple-IN keeps every row from the first selling year

dialogue.text: Give each product's first selling year with that year's quantity and price.
I find MIN(year) per product and keep rows matching the (product_id, year) tuple, which preserves multiple sales in that first year.
Why not just GROUP BY and MIN the columns?
MIN(quantity)/MIN(price) wouldn't be tied to the first year's actual row; the tuple match keeps the correct values.

followUpsQA.a: RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY year) then filter rank = 1.
RANK keeps all first-year rows; ROW_NUMBER would arbitrarily keep one.

questions.q: Classes More Than 5 Students. Courses(student, class), PK (student, class). List classes that have at least 5 students.

answer.oneLiner: Group by class and keep groups whose student count is at least 5 with HAVING.
keyPoints: HAVING filters aggregated groups, WHERE can't.
PK means (student,class) is unique, so COUNT(student) = distinct students.

code.snippet:
SELECT class
FROM Courses
GROUP BY class
HAVING COUNT(student) >= 5;
code.note: HAVING for post-aggregation filtering

dialogue.text: Which classes have 5 or more students?
Group by class and keep those with COUNT(student) >= 5 via HAVING.
Why HAVING and not WHERE?
WHERE filters individual rows before grouping; the count only exists after aggregation, so HAVING is required.

followUpsQA.a: Not here — the composite PK already makes each (student,class) unique.
WHERE is pre-aggregation per row; HAVING is post-aggregation per group.

questions.q: Find Followers Count. Followers(user_id, follower_id), PK (user_id, follower_id). For each user output the number of followers, ordered by user_id.

answer.oneLiner: Group by user_id and COUNT the follower rows; order by user_id.
keyPoints: Composite PK guarantees no duplicate follower, so plain COUNT works.
ORDER BY user_id for the required ordering.

code.snippet:
SELECT user_id, COUNT(follower_id) AS followers_count
FROM Followers
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id;
code.note: Straight COUNT per group

dialogue.text: Count followers per user, sorted by user_id.
Group by user_id with COUNT(follower_id) and ORDER BY user_id.
Any risk of double counting?
No — the (user_id, follower_id) primary key prevents duplicate follower rows.

followUpsQA.a: Only if duplicates were allowed; the PK rules that out here.
They won't appear; you'd need a left join from a Users table to include them.

questions.q: Biggest Single Number. MyNumbers(num) — may contain duplicates. Return the largest number that appears exactly once, or NULL if none.

answer.oneLiner: Keep values with COUNT = 1 in a subquery, then take MAX; MAX over an empty set yields NULL automatically.
keyPoints: HAVING COUNT(*) = 1 isolates the singletons.
Wrapping in MAX returns NULL when there are no singletons.

code.snippet:
SELECT MAX(num) AS num
FROM (
    SELECT num
    FROM MyNumbers
    GROUP BY num
    HAVING COUNT(*) = 1
) t;
code.note: MAX of empty set => NULL, satisfying the edge case

dialogue.text: Largest number appearing exactly once, else NULL.
I group by num, keep those with COUNT = 1, then MAX the result; the MAX wrapper gives NULL when none qualify.
Why does the NULL case work without extra handling?
Aggregating MAX over zero rows returns NULL by definition, which is exactly the required output.

followUpsQA.q: Why the subquery instead of HAVING + ORDER BY LIMIT 1?
followUpsQA.a: ORDER BY LIMIT 1 returns no row (not NULL) when empty; MAX guarantees a NULL row.

followUpsQA.q: Could you use a window function?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY num), but it's heavier than GROUP BY here.

questions.q: Customers Who Bought All Products. Customer(customer_id, product_key), Product(product_key PK). Find customers who bought every product.

answer.oneLiner: Group by customer and keep those whose distinct product count equals the total number of products (relational division).
keyPoints: COUNT(DISTINCT product_key) per customer.
Compare to (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Product).
DISTINCT guards against a customer buying the same product twice.

code.snippet:
SELECT customer_id
FROM Customer
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT product_key) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Product);
code.note: Classic relational division

dialogue.text: Which customers bought all products?
Per customer I count distinct products and keep those matching the total product count — relational division.
Why COUNT(DISTINCT ...)?
A customer may buy the same product multiple times; DISTINCT ensures we compare unique products to the catalog size.

followUpsQA.a: Finding rows on one side that match every value on the other — 'for all' semantics.
Customers with no product that they haven't bought — a double-negation form of the same idea.

questions.q: The Number of Employees Which Report to Each Employee. Employees(employee_id PK, name, manager_id, age). For each manager with at least one direct report, output reports_count and the rounded average age of reports, ordered by employee_id.

answer.oneLiner: Self-join managers to their reports on manager_id = employee_id, then aggregate count and average age per manager.
keyPoints: Two aliases: m (manager) and e (report).
INNER JOIN naturally excludes managers with zero reports.
ROUND(AVG(e.age)) for the average age.

code.snippet:
SELECT m.employee_id,
       m.name,
       COUNT(e.employee_id) AS reports_count,
       ROUND(AVG(e.age))    AS average_age
FROM Employees m
JOIN Employees e ON e.manager_id = m.employee_id
GROUP BY m.employee_id, m.name
ORDER BY m.employee_id;
code.note: Self-join + aggregate

dialogue.text: For each manager, how many report to them and their reports' average age?
I self-join Employees, matching each report's manager_id to the manager's employee_id, then COUNT and AVG per manager.
How are managers with no reports excluded?
The INNER JOIN only produces rows where a report exists, so zero-report managers drop out automatically.

followUpsQA.a: The problem expects a whole-number average; ROUND() to default 0 decimals.
The INNER JOIN — only managers with matching reports survive.

questions.q: Primary Department for Each Employee. Employee(employee_id, department_id, primary_flag), PK (employee_id, department_id). An employee in a single department has it as primary regardless of the flag; otherwise the row with primary_flag = 'Y' is primary. Report each employee's primary department.

answer.oneLiner: UNION the explicitly flagged 'Y' rows with employees who have exactly one department (so no flag is set).
keyPoints: Branch 1: primary_flag = 'Y'.
Branch 2: employees with COUNT(*) = 1 department.
UNION dedupes the rare overlap.

code.snippet:
SELECT employee_id, department_id
FROM Employee
WHERE primary_flag = 'Y'
UNION
SELECT employee_id, department_id
FROM Employee
GROUP BY employee_id
HAVING COUNT(*) = 1;
code.note: Two cases combined with UNION

dialogue.text: Find each employee's primary department, given the single-department rule.
I take rows flagged 'Y', then UNION employees who belong to exactly one department since those have no explicit flag.
Why does the GROUP BY branch return department_id safely?
Because COUNT(*) = 1 means there's a single row per employee, so the bare department_id is unambiguous.

followUpsQA.q: Why UNION rather than UNION ALL?
followUpsQA.a: To dedupe in case a single-department employee is also flagged 'Y'.
COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY employee_id) and pick rows where flag='Y' OR the count is 1.

questions.q: Triangle Judgement. Triangle(x, y, z). For each row decide whether the three lengths can form a triangle (output 'Yes'/'No').

answer.oneLiner: Apply the triangle inequality (each pair's sum exceeds the third side) inside a CASE expression.
keyPoints: All three conditions must hold: x+y>z, x+z>y, y+z>x.
CASE returns 'Yes'/'No' per row.

code.snippet:
SELECT x, y, z,
       CASE WHEN x + y > z AND x + z > y AND y + z > x
            THEN 'Yes' ELSE 'No' END AS triangle
FROM Triangle;
code.note: Triangle inequality in a CASE

dialogue.text: Can each triple form a triangle?
Yes only if every pair of sides sums to more than the third, expressed with three AND-ed comparisons in a CASE.
Is checking the two smaller sides against the largest enough?
Logically yes, but without sorting I just test all three pairs, which is equivalent and simpler in SQL.

followUpsQA.a: Without sorting you don't know which side is largest, so testing all three is safe.
Strict > correctly rejects collinear points as 'No'.

questions.q: Consecutive Numbers. Logs(id PK, num). Find all numbers that appear at least three times consecutively (by increasing id).

answer.oneLiner: Self-join the table to its next two ids and require all three to share the same num.
keyPoints: Join l2 on id+1 and l3 on id+2.
All three nums equal => a run of 3.
DISTINCT collapses overlapping windows of the same value.

code.snippet:
SELECT DISTINCT l1.num AS ConsecutiveNums
FROM Logs l1
JOIN Logs l2 ON l2.id = l1.id + 1 AND l2.num = l1.num
JOIN Logs l3 ON l3.id = l1.id + 2 AND l3.num = l1.num;
code.note: Triple self-join on consecutive ids

dialogue.text: Which numbers appear three times in a row?
I self-join each row to the next two ids and require identical num across all three, then DISTINCT the result.
Does this assume ids are gap-free?
Yes — it relies on id+1/id+2 being contiguous; with gaps I'd use LEAD()/window framing instead.

followUpsQA.a: Compare num to LEAD(num,1) and LEAD(num,2) OVER (ORDER BY id).
Track a 'group' via row_number minus a per-value row_number (gaps-and-islands).

questions.q: Product Price at a Given Date. Products(product_id, new_price, change_date), PK (product_id, change_date). Find the price of every product on 2019-08-16; products with no change on/before that date default to 10.

answer.oneLiner: For each product take the latest change on or before the target date; COALESCE to 10 when there's none.
keyPoints: Latest applicable change = MAX(change_date) <= target, per product.
COALESCE(..., 10) supplies the default starting price.
Drive the list from DISTINCT product_id so untouched products still appear.

code.snippet:
SELECT p.product_id,
       COALESCE((
           SELECT pr.new_price
           FROM Products pr
           WHERE pr.product_id = p.product_id
             AND pr.change_date <= '2019-08-16'
           ORDER BY pr.change_date DESC
           LIMIT 1
       ), 10) AS price
FROM (SELECT DISTINCT product_id FROM Products) p;
code.note: Latest-change-before-date with a default

dialogue.text: Price of each product on 2019-08-16, defaulting to 10.
Per product I grab the most recent change_date on or before the target; if none exists, COALESCE supplies 10.
Why iterate over DISTINCT product_id separately?
So products whose only changes are after the date — or which I'd otherwise miss — still show up with the default price.

followUpsQA.a: RANK by change_date DESC within products filtered to <= date, then union the never-changed defaults.
It picks the latest change effective on the target date.

questions.q: Last Person to Fit in the Bus. Queue(person_id, person_name, weight, turn). People board in turn order; the bus holds 1000 kg. Return the name of the last person who can board.

answer.oneLiner: Compute a running weight by boarding order, keep rows where the cumulative weight is within 1000, and take the heaviest cumulative (last to board).
keyPoints: SUM(weight) OVER (ORDER BY turn) is the running total.
Filter cum <= 1000.
The max cumulative among survivors is the last person on.

code.snippet:
SELECT person_name
FROM (
    SELECT person_name,
           SUM(weight) OVER (ORDER BY turn) AS cum
    FROM Queue
) t
WHERE cum <= 1000
ORDER BY cum DESC
LIMIT 1;
code.note: Running total via window frame

dialogue.text: Who is the last person able to board within 1000 kg?
I build a running weight ordered by turn, keep rows at or under 1000, and pick the one with the largest cumulative — the last to fit.
Why does ORDER BY cum DESC LIMIT 1 give the last boarder?
The valid boarders form a prefix of the queue; the maximum cumulative weight among them is exactly the final person admitted.

followUpsQA.a: With ORDER BY, the default frame is RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING TO CURRENT ROW — fine here since turns are unique.
The result is empty; the lightest first person normally fits, so it's rarely empty.

questions.q: Count Salary Categories. Accounts(account_id, income). Count accounts in three buckets: Low (< 20000), Average ([20000, 50000]), High (> 50000). All three categories must appear even with a count of 0.

answer.oneLiner: UNION ALL three one-row aggregates, each summing a boolean range test, so every category appears regardless of count.
keyPoints: SUM(income < 20000) etc. counts rows in each band.
UNION ALL guarantees all three category rows exist.
BETWEEN is inclusive for the Average band.

code.snippet:
SELECT 'Low Salary' AS category, SUM(income < 20000) AS accounts_count
FROM Accounts
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Average Salary', SUM(income BETWEEN 20000 AND 50000)
FROM Accounts
UNION ALL
SELECT 'High Salary', SUM(income > 50000)
FROM Accounts;
code.note: Fixed category rows via UNION ALL

dialogue.text: Count accounts per salary band, always showing all three bands.
I write three one-row queries, each SUMming a boolean band test, and UNION ALL them so empty bands still report 0.
Why not GROUP BY a CASE category?
GROUP BY only emits bands that have rows, so an empty category would vanish; the UNION ALL skeleton avoids that.

followUpsQA.a: The three category labels are distinct, and ALL avoids a needless dedupe sort.
Yes, so 20000 and 50000 fall into Average as required.

questions.q: Employees Whose Manager Left the Company. Employees(employee_id PK, name, manager_id, salary). Find employees earning under 30000 whose manager_id is non-null but no longer exists in the table. Order by employee_id.

answer.oneLiner: Filter salary < 30000 and a non-null manager_id that is absent from the set of existing employee_ids.
keyPoints: manager_id IS NOT NULL excludes top-level employees.
manager_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id ...) is the 'manager left' test.
employee_id values are non-null, so NOT IN is safe.

code.snippet:
SELECT employee_id
FROM Employees
WHERE salary < 30000
  AND manager_id IS NOT NULL
  AND manager_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM Employees)
ORDER BY employee_id;
code.note: Anti-join via NOT IN on a non-null key

dialogue.text: Low-paid employees whose manager has left.
I keep salary < 30000, require a non-null manager_id, and check that manager_id isn't among current employee_ids.
Is NOT IN safe here given NULL pitfalls?
Yes — employee_id is the primary key so the subquery has no NULLs, which is what makes NOT IN reliable.

followUpsQA.q: Why is NOT IN risky in general?
followUpsQA.a: If the subquery returns any NULL, NOT IN yields no rows; a PK column avoids that.
LEFT JOIN self on manager and keep rows where the manager row IS NULL.

questions.q: Exchange Seats. Seat(id PK, student) with consecutive ids. Swap seats of every pair of adjacent students; if the count is odd, the last student stays put.

answer.oneLiner: Remap ids by parity: odd id moves to id+1, even moves to id-1, except a final odd id (the max) stays.
keyPoints: Odd id and not the last => id + 1.
Even id => id - 1.
Odd id equal to MAX(id) => unchanged.
ORDER BY the computed id.

code.snippet:
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN id % 2 = 1 AND id = (SELECT MAX(id) FROM Seat) THEN id
        WHEN id % 2 = 1 THEN id + 1
        ELSE id - 1
    END AS id,
    student
FROM Seat
ORDER BY id;
code.note: Parity remap with an odd-tail exception

dialogue.text: Swap adjacent students' seats, leaving a trailing odd seat in place.
I recompute id by parity — odd to id+1, even to id-1 — but a final odd id keeps its value, then order by the new id.
How do you detect the trailing odd seat?
It's the odd id equal to MAX(id); that branch returns id unchanged.

followUpsQA.q: Why ORDER BY the new id?
followUpsQA.a: Swapping only changes the id label; the output must be sorted by the final seat number.
Use COALESCE(LEAD/LAG(student) OVER (ORDER BY id), student) to pull the neighbor's name.

questions.q: Movie Rating. Movies(movie_id, title), Users(user_id, name), MovieRating(movie_id, user_id, rating, created_at). Output (1) the user who rated the most movies — ties broken by lexicographically smaller name — and (2) the movie with the highest average rating in Feb 2020 — ties broken by lexicographically smaller title.

answer.oneLiner: Two ranked one-row queries UNION ALL'd: top rater by count then name, top Feb-2020 movie by average rating then title.
keyPoints: Part 1: GROUP BY user, ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC, name ASC, LIMIT 1.
Part 2: filter created_at to Feb 2020, GROUP BY movie, ORDER BY AVG(rating) DESC, title ASC, LIMIT 1.
UNION ALL stacks both single-column results.

code.snippet:
(SELECT u.name AS results
 FROM MovieRating mr
 JOIN Users u ON u.user_id = mr.user_id
 GROUP BY mr.user_id
 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC, u.name
 LIMIT 1)
UNION ALL
(SELECT m.title
 FROM MovieRating mr
 JOIN Movies m ON m.movie_id = mr.movie_id
 WHERE mr.created_at >= '2020-02-01' AND mr.created_at < '2020-03-01'
 GROUP BY mr.movie_id
 ORDER BY AVG(mr.rating) DESC, m.title
 LIMIT 1);
code.note: Two ranked queries combined

dialogue.text: Find the busiest rater and February's best-rated movie.
Two parts: top rater by rating count then name; and the Feb-2020 movie with the highest average rating, ties by title — both LIMIT 1, combined with UNION ALL.
Why parenthesize each SELECT?
So each ORDER BY/LIMIT binds to its own subquery rather than to the whole UNION.

followUpsQA.a: The two strings could coincide; ALL preserves both and skips a dedupe.
created_at < '2020-03-01' cleanly includes all of February regardless of time-of-day.

questions.q: Restaurant Growth. Customer(customer_id, name, visited_on, amount). Compute the 7-day moving total and 7-day moving average (rounded to 2 decimals) of daily amounts, starting from the 7th day onward, ordered by date.

answer.oneLiner: Aggregate to one row per day, sum each day with the prior six via a self-join (or window frame), and only emit days at least 6 days after the first.
keyPoints: Collapse to daily totals first (multiple customers per day).
Sum the trailing 7-day window per day.
Skip the first 6 days where a full window doesn't exist; divide by 7 for the average.

code.snippet:
SELECT visited_on, amount, ROUND(amount / 7, 2) AS average_amount
FROM (
    SELECT a.visited_on,
           SUM(b.amount) AS amount,
           DATEDIFF(a.visited_on, (SELECT MIN(visited_on) FROM Customer)) AS day_diff
    FROM (SELECT visited_on, SUM(amount) AS amount FROM Customer GROUP BY visited_on) a
    JOIN Customer b
      ON b.visited_on BETWEEN DATE_SUB(a.visited_on, INTERVAL 6 DAY) AND a.visited_on
    GROUP BY a.visited_on
) t
WHERE day_diff >= 6
ORDER BY visited_on;
code.note: Trailing 7-day window, first full window on day 7

dialogue.text: Give the 7-day moving sum and average of daily revenue.
I first total amount per day, then for each day sum the window of that day and the six before it, emitting only days at least 6 days after the first.
Why start on the 7th day?
Earlier days don't have a full 7-day history, so a moving average would be based on fewer than 7 days.

followUpsQA.a: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY visited_on ROWS 6 PRECEDING) over the daily-totals CTE, then filter the first 6 days.

followUpsQA.q: Why aggregate to daily totals first?
followUpsQA.a: Multiple customers can visit the same day; the window must operate on per-day sums.

questions.q: Friend Requests II: Who Has the Most Friends. RequestAccepted(requester_id, accepter_id, accept_date). A friendship counts for both people. Return the id with the most friends and that count (single answer guaranteed).

answer.oneLiner: Treat both columns as friend endpoints by UNION ALL-ing them into one id column, then group and take the top count.
keyPoints: UNION ALL requester_id and accepter_id into a single id list.
GROUP BY id, COUNT(*) = number of friendships.
ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT 1.

code.snippet:
SELECT id, COUNT(*) AS num
FROM (
    SELECT requester_id AS id FROM RequestAccepted
    UNION ALL
    SELECT accepter_id      FROM RequestAccepted
) t
GROUP BY id
ORDER BY num DESC
LIMIT 1;
code.note: Flatten both endpoints, then count

dialogue.text: Who has the most friends?
Each accepted request gives both people a friend, so I UNION ALL the two id columns, group, count, and take the maximum.
Why UNION ALL and not UNION?
UNION would dedupe identical ids across the two halves and undercount; ALL keeps every endpoint occurrence.

followUpsQA.a: LIMIT 1 returns one; to show all leaders you'd compare against the max count instead.

followUpsQA.q: Does direction of the request matter?
followUpsQA.a: No — friendship is mutual, so both endpoints count equally.

questions.q: Investments in 2016. Insurance(pid, tiv_2015, tiv_2016, lat, lon). Sum tiv_2016 for policyholders whose tiv_2015 is shared with at least one other holder AND whose (lat, lon) location is unique. Round to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: Keep rows whose tiv_2015 appears more than once and whose (lat,lon) appears exactly once, then SUM tiv_2016.
keyPoints: Shared tiv_2015: GROUP BY tiv_2015 HAVING COUNT(*) > 1.
Unique location: GROUP BY lat, lon HAVING COUNT(*) = 1.
Combine both as IN filters, then SUM and ROUND.

code.note: Two HAVING-based membership filters

dialogue.text: Total 2016 investment for the qualifying policyholders.
I select rows whose tiv_2015 is shared (count > 1) and whose location is unique (count = 1), then SUM tiv_2016.
Why a composite (lat, lon) in the second IN?
Uniqueness is about the location pair, not either coordinate alone, so I group by both columns together.

followUpsQA.a: COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY tiv_2015) > 1 and COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY lat,lon) = 1.
Monetary totals are expected to two decimal places per the spec.

questions.q: Department Top Three Salaries. Employee(id, name, salary, departmentId), Department(id, name). List employees who are among the top three distinct salaries in their department (high earners).

answer.oneLiner: DENSE_RANK salaries within each department descending and keep ranks 1–3, then join to department names.
keyPoints: DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY departmentId ORDER BY salary DESC).
DENSE_RANK so tied salaries share a rank (top-3 distinct salaries).
Filter rank <= 3 and join Department for the name.

code.snippet:
SELECT d.name AS Department, e.name AS Employee, e.salary AS Salary
FROM (
    SELECT name, salary, departmentId,
           DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY departmentId ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
    FROM Employee
) e
JOIN Department d ON d.id = e.departmentId
WHERE e.rk <= 3;
code.note: Top-3 distinct salaries via DENSE_RANK

dialogue.text: Show the top three earners per department.
I DENSE_RANK salaries within each department descending, keep ranks up to 3, and join Department for its name.
Why DENSE_RANK rather than ROW_NUMBER?
Two employees on the same salary should share a rank and both count toward 'top three salaries'; ROW_NUMBER would split them.

followUpsQA.a: Yes — equal top salaries both get rank 1 with DENSE_RANK, so more than three rows can appear.
RANK leaves gaps after ties, which would shrink the top-3 distinct salaries; DENSE_RANK doesn't.

questions.q: Fix Names in a Table. Users(user_id PK, name). Normalize each name so the first letter is uppercase and the rest lowercase. Order by user_id.

answer.oneLiner: Concatenate the uppercased first character with the lowercased remainder.
keyPoints: UPPER(LEFT(name,1)) for the initial.
LOWER(SUBSTRING(name,2)) for the tail.
CONCAT joins them; ORDER BY user_id.

code.snippet:
SELECT user_id,
       CONCAT(UPPER(LEFT(name, 1)), LOWER(SUBSTRING(name, 2))) AS name
FROM Users
ORDER BY user_id;
code.note: Title-case with LEFT/SUBSTRING

dialogue.text: Capitalize the first letter and lowercase the rest of each name.
I CONCAT the uppercased first character with the lowercased substring from position 2 onward, ordered by user_id.
Does SUBSTRING from position 2 handle single-character names?
Yes — for a one-letter name the substring is empty, so the result is just the uppercased letter.

followUpsQA.a: SQL SUBSTRING is 1-based, so position 2 starts at the second character.
UPPER/LOWER are locale-dependent; for ASCII names here it's straightforward.

questions.q: Patients With a Condition. Patients(patient_id, patient_name, conditions) where conditions is a space-separated list of codes. Find patients who have Type I Diabetes, whose code starts with the prefix 'DIAB1'.

answer.oneLiner: Match the prefix at the start of the string or after a space, so 'DIAB1...' is found but not a code that merely contains DIAB1 mid-token.
keyPoints: conditions LIKE 'DIAB1%' covers the first token.
OR conditions LIKE '% DIAB1%' covers later tokens.
The leading space prevents matching e.g. 'SADIAB1' substrings.

code.snippet:
SELECT patient_id, patient_name, conditions
FROM Patients
WHERE conditions LIKE 'DIAB1%'
   OR conditions LIKE '% DIAB1%';
code.note: Anchor the prefix to a word boundary

dialogue.text: Find patients with a Type I diabetes code (DIAB1 prefix).
I match conditions LIKE 'DIAB1%' for the first code or '% DIAB1%' for a later code, anchoring to a word boundary.
Why the leading space in the second pattern?
Without it, a code like 'XDIAB1' would falsely match; the space ensures DIAB1 begins a token.

followUpsQA.a: DIAB1 can be the first token (no preceding space) or a later one (preceded by a space).
conditions REGEXP '(^|\\s)DIAB1' captures both cases in one predicate.

questions.q: Delete Duplicate Emails. Person(id PK, email). Delete duplicate emails, keeping only the row with the smallest id per email. (This is a DELETE, not a SELECT.)

answer.oneLiner: Self-join Person on equal email and a larger id, then delete the higher-id side so the minimum id survives.
keyPoints: Join condition: same email, p1.id > p2.id.
DELETE p1 removes every non-minimal duplicate.
Only the smallest id per email remains.

code.snippet:
DELETE p1
FROM Person p1
JOIN Person p2
  ON p1.email = p2.email AND p1.id > p2.id;
code.note: Delete the higher-id duplicates

dialogue.text: Remove duplicate emails, keeping the lowest id.
I self-join on equal email where p1.id is greater, then DELETE p1 — that strips every duplicate except the minimum id.
Why is the smallest id guaranteed to survive?
A row is only deleted if some smaller-id row shares its email; the minimum has none smaller, so it's never deleted.

followUpsQA.a: DELETE rows where ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY id) > 1.
p1 is the higher-id alias, so naming it deletes the duplicates, not the keeper.

questions.q: Second Highest Salary. Employee(id PK, salary). Return the second highest distinct salary, or NULL if it doesn't exist.

answer.oneLiner: Order distinct salaries descending and take OFFSET 1; wrapping in a subquery makes the empty case return NULL instead of no row.
keyPoints: DISTINCT so duplicate top salaries don't occupy the second slot.
LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1 picks the runner-up.
The outer SELECT (...) yields NULL when there's no second salary.

code.note: Subquery wrapper guarantees a NULL row

dialogue.text: Return the second highest distinct salary, NULL if none.
I take DISTINCT salaries ordered descending with LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1, wrapped in a scalar subquery so the empty case is NULL.
Why the outer SELECT wrapper?
A bare LIMIT/OFFSET returns zero rows when there's no second salary; wrapping it produces a single NULL row as required.

followUpsQA.a: Without it, two top-paid employees would make the second row equal the maximum.
Use OFFSET N-1, or DENSE_RANK() = N.

questions.q: Group Sold Products By The Date. Activities(sell_date, product) — may contain duplicates. For each date, report the number of distinct products sold and a comma-separated, alphabetically sorted list of those product names.

answer.oneLiner: Group by date, COUNT(DISTINCT product) for the tally, and GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT product ORDER BY product) for the sorted list.
keyPoints: DISTINCT inside both aggregates dedupes repeated sales.
GROUP_CONCAT ... ORDER BY product gives alphabetical names.
ORDER BY sell_date for output order.

code.snippet:
SELECT sell_date,
       COUNT(DISTINCT product) AS num_sold,
       GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT product ORDER BY product SEPARATOR ',') AS products
FROM Activities
GROUP BY sell_date
ORDER BY sell_date;
code.note: GROUP_CONCAT with DISTINCT + ORDER BY

dialogue.text: Per date, count distinct products and list them alphabetically.
Group by sell_date, COUNT(DISTINCT product), and GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT product ORDER BY product) for the comma-separated list.
Why DISTINCT inside GROUP_CONCAT?
The table can repeat a product on a date; DISTINCT ensures each name appears once in the list and the count.

followUpsQA.a: Postgres uses STRING_AGG(product, ',' ORDER BY product); SQL Server uses STRING_AGG too.
MySQL GROUP_CONCAT defaults to comma, but stating SEPARATOR ',' is explicit.

questions.q: Find Users With Valid Emails. Users(user_id, name, mail). A valid email's prefix may contain letters, digits, underscore, dot, or dash, must start with a letter, and is followed by the literal domain '@leetcode.com'. Return the valid rows.

answer.oneLiner: Validate with a single anchored regular expression: a leading letter, allowed prefix characters, then the exact @leetcode.com domain.
keyPoints: ^[A-Za-z] forces a leading letter.
[A-Za-z0-9_.-]* allows the rest of the prefix.
@leetcode\.com$ matches the literal domain (escaped dot), anchored to the end.

code.note: Anchored regex; escape the dot in the domain

dialogue.text: Keep only the rows with valid @leetcode.com emails.
One anchored REGEXP: start with a letter, allow letters/digits/underscore/dot/dash in the prefix, then the literal @leetcode.com to the end.
Why escape the dot in the domain?
An unescaped '.' matches any character, so '\\.' forces a literal dot, preventing things like '@leetcodeXcom'.

followUpsQA.a: Without anchors, a partial match anywhere in the string would wrongly validate.

followUpsQA.q: Why put '-' last in the character class?
followUpsQA.a: A trailing dash is treated literally rather than as a range operator.
